abstract:
The transfer coefficient ($\alpha$) serves as a fundamental parameter in the kinetic analysis of electrode processes. Current IUPAC definitions for the cathodic ($\alpha_c$) and anodic ($\alpha_a$) transfer coefficients include the total number of electrons ($n$) within the derivative of the logarithm of the current density with respect to potential. This report evaluates the practical and theoretical limitations of such definitions, noting that they strictly apply only to single elementary steps involving the simultaneous transfer of $n$ electrons.

Drawing on Marcus’s theory of electron transfer, the authors argue that the simultaneous transfer of multiple electrons is energetically unfavorable and highly improbable compared to sequential one-electron steps. Consequently, treating most multistep, multi-electron electrode reactions as single-step processes leads to significant mechanistic errors. A common fallacy in the literature involves equating experimental Tafel slopes to $\alpha n$ and attempting to derive $n$ by assuming an arbitrary value for $\alpha$ (typically 0.5), which often results in physically inconsistent interpretations of the rate-determining step.

To rectify these inconsistencies, this report proposes a revised definition of the transfer coefficient that is independent of reaction stoichiometry or mechanism. By defining $\alpha_c$ and $\alpha_a$ purely in terms of the experimental Tafel slope—specifically as $-(RT/F)(\text{d}\ln|j_c|/\text{d}E)$ and $(RT/F)(\text{d}\ln j_a/\text{d}E)$, respectively—the coefficient becomes a directly measurable quantity. This approach ensures that the transfer coefficient remains an unbiased experimental probe, allowing for more robust subsequent mechanistic modeling of complex electrochemical systems.
